title: Scottish tablet
subtitle: Grainy, crumbly sugar confection made with condensed milk and butter
excerpt: Scottish tablet is not fudge: it is drier, harder, and grainier. It is a confection from condensed milk, sugar, butter, and whole milk boiled to 116°C, then beaten aggressively until it grains and sets. The texture should crumble when you bite it, with a clean, very sweet dairy flavour.
sourceNotes: Scottish tablet is a traditional Scottish confection documented in domestic cookbooks from the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. It appears in Meg Dods, The Cook and Housewife's Manual (1826), a Scottish domestic cookery text available in public-domain archives, and is referenced in later Scottish domestic guides as a standard confection of the region. The key distinction from fudge (a coarser, harder grain) is achieved by a higher cooking temperature and more vigorous beating. The condensed-milk version became the standard in the twentieth century; the historical version used fresh milk and butter in similar proportions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a sealed box for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Set overnight at room temperature. Cut the next day. Stores in a sealed tin for up to 3 weeks.

glossaryTerms.definition: Tablet's characteristic crumbly texture comes from rapid, vigorous beating once the mixture comes off the heat. Unlike fudge, where gentle beating produces fine crystals, tablet is beaten hard from the start, which promotes coarser, faster crystallisation. The mixture sets quickly once graining begins — pour it into the tin within 2 to 3 minutes of the texture changing from glossy to rough. Tablet that is poured too late is so thick it tears rather than levels; tablet poured too soon is still liquid. The transition is recognisable: the mixture suddenly thickens, loses its gloss, and starts to pull away from the sides of the pan.

content.text: Boiling sugar confectionery is hot and sticky. Use a large, heavy saucepan to prevent the mixture from boiling over. Keep children out of the kitchen during the cooking stage.
Tablet is a distinctly Scottish sweet. The texture (coarser and more crumbly than fudge) is the point, not a failure. It keeps well and is one of the traditional sweets brought out at Hogmanay.

Combine the {{granulated-sugar}}, {{condensed-milk}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, and {{whole-milk}} in a large, heavy saucepan. Stir over low heat until the sugar dissolves completely. Increase to medium and clip on the thermometer. Stir frequently to prevent scorching. Cook to 116°C (soft-ball stage). Remove from the heat and add the {{vanilla-extract}} if using.
Begin beating immediately with a wooden spoon, working hard and fast. Read the
note: as soon as the mixture starts to thicken, lose its gloss, and pull away from the pan, pour into a greased and lined 20 cm square tin without delay. Level with a spatula.

After 5 minutes, score lines across the surface with a knife to mark the squares before the tablet fully hardens. Leave at room temperature for at least 1 hour. Cut along the scored lines with a sharp knife and store in a sealed tin.
